front: Sculpture: giant lipstick on tank treads at Yale University
Sculpture: originally had inflatable red vinyl balloon top, later replaced with steel
Sculpture: tank-shaped base topped by red-tipped vertical tube, in Morse College courtyard at Yale

front: Sculpture: cherry designed by Coosje van Bruggen, inspired by Versailles
back: Spoonbridge and Cherry (Claes Oldenburg & Coosje van Bruggen)

front: Sculpture: enormous hamburger made of rubber and canvas; topped with foam pickle slice

back: Dropped Cone (Claes Oldenburg & Coosje van Bruggen)

front: Sculpture: four giant white feather pieces at Nelson-Atkins Museum in Kansas City
Sculpture: inspired by a Native American headdress in a Frederic Remington painting
Sculpture: must be repainted yearly
Artist: operated the Ray Gun Manufacturing Company shopfront, also called 'The Store'
Artist: exhibited found objects in the Mouse Museum, a black box shaped like Mickey Mouse
Artist: involved in non-verbal artistic performances called 'Happenings'; many performed at his Ray Gun Theater
Artist: taught by Allan Kaprow, the inventor of Happenings
Artist: first one-man show at Judson Memorial Church (which also hosted Carolee Schneemann's Meat Joy)
Artist: staged The Course of the Knife at Venice's Arsenale in 1986
Sculpture: weathering steel piece for SEPTA City Hall Station embedding the number '76' into a silvery spring
Artist: 'soft' sculptures of distorted household objects (toilet, shuttlecock, bathtub, screw) alongside 'hard' giant versions of everyday objects
Sculpture: giant BLT sandwich in the Whitney Museum
Artist: bookplate features a spilled ink-bottle splashing across a notebook page
Sculpture: Batcolumn, a gray steel lattice baseball bat in Chicago
Sculpture: giant binoculars at entrance of Chiat/Day Building (Binoculars Building), designed with Frank Gehry
Sculpture: Free Stamp in Willard Park, Cleveland
Artist: proposed replacing Trafalgar Square's Nelson's Column with a 'Colossal Monument' of a 'Gearshift in Motion'
Artist: scribbled crude Mickey Mouse for Forrest Myers' Moon Museum (ceramic wafer on Apollo 12 Lunar Module)
Artist: modeled 'profile' of a Chrysler product with polyurethane, inspired by a children's toy
